preamble: The shift from the voiceless alveolar stop [t] to the voiceless dental fricative [θ] is one of the most widely documented consonant changes in historical phonology. It occurs via two main pathways: spirantization (weakening of a stop to a fricative, often in intervocalic or post-vocalic position) and Grimm's Law-type chain shifts in which an entire class of voiceless stops acquires friction. The change is attested across Indo-European, Semitic, and Berber families, reflecting independent but phonetically motivated convergence.

examples.note: Grimm's Law (First Germanic Consonant Shift, c. 500–200 BCE): PIE voiceless stops *p *t *k shifted to fricatives *f *θ *x in Proto-Germanic. The *t → *θ arm is the most visible, preserved as ⟨th⟩ [θ] in Gothic and Old English. Latin tres vs. English three exemplifies the change.
The coda *t → *θ is visible in Gothic ⟨þ⟩ and in the English spelling ⟨th⟩. Compare Latin dens (genitive dentis), which preserves the original stop.

Iranian languages underwent a 'partial Grimm's Law': PIE voiceless stops *p *t *k shifted to fricatives *f *θ *x before consonants. Old Persian θata- preserves *θ from earlier *t, while western Middle Iranian later lost θ almost entirely. The PIE palatal *ḱ → Indo-Iranian *ś → Old Persian θ shows a parallel sibilant-to-fricative path.

The preconsonantal spirantization *t → θ is systematic in Old Iranian. Avestan θrī- and Old Persian θri- correspond directly to Latin trēs and Greek τρεῖς, with the Iranian reflex showing the fricativized stop.

Old Irish lenition: /t/ in intervocalic position or after certain proclitic words shifted to [θ]. This is written ⟨th⟩ in Old Irish manuscripts and represents a true voiceless dental fricative. The lenited form of túath is [θuaθ]. Modern Irish later shifted lenited /t/ to [h], losing the fricative stage, but the Old Irish [θ] stage is well documented.

The lenition of voiceless stops in Old Irish (producing voiceless fricatives rather than the voiced stops found in Brittonic) is dated to early Primitive Irish, before the Goidelic-Brittonic split solidified. The [θ] pronunciation is attested in the Ogham inscriptions and early manuscript tradition.

A systematic shift in Koine Greek where aspirated stops /pʰ tʰ kʰ/ weakened into the fricatives /f θ x/. The letter Theta (Θ), originally pronounced like the 't' in 'top', became the 'th' in 'thin'. This process was completed by the early centuries AD.
While Standard Armenian preserves the aspirated stop [tʰ], some dialects like Agulis have undergone spirantization, shifting the aspirated dental stop into a true dental fricative [θ].

Tiberian Hebrew Begedkefat rule: the six stops /b g d k p t/ spirantize to fricatives after a vowel in non-geminate position. Post-vocalic /t/ → [θ] (the Hebrew letter tāv without dagesh). The pattern is systematic and affects all six stops in parallel environments.

In Kabyle and related Northern Berber varieties (including Middle Atlas Tamazight), lax (non-geminate, non-post-nasal) /t/ spirantizes to [θ] in intervocalic and post-vocalic positions. Geminate /tt/ and post-nasal /nt/ are immune. The shift is absent in Tuareg and Zenaga, making it a Northern Berber areal feature.

sources: Kossmann, M. G. & Stroomer, H. J. (1997). Berber Phonology. In Kaye (ed.), Phonologies of Asia and Africa. Eisenbrauns.
Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction. MIT Press.
Fortson, B. W. (2010). Indo-European Language and Culture: An Introduction. Wiley-Blackwell.
Thurneysen, R. (1946). A Grammar of Old Irish. Dublin Institute for Advanced Studies.
Horrocks, G. (2010). Greek: A History of the Language and its Speakers.
Vaux, B. (1998). The Phonology of Armenian.

related.label: Subsequent step: Sibilantization (θ → s, as in Modern Irish)
Voiced parallel: d → ð (voiced dental fricative)
Part of Grimm's Law: p → f
Part of Grimm's Law: k → h
